claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask: Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

(For example, this could include school, college, A-levels, T-levels, or certain approved training courses.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. A few more questions to assess your eligibility:

1. Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great. And do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000? 

(This won't affect whether you're eligible for Child Benefit, but it may mean you need to pay a tax charge.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure about the exact threshold, but could you tell me - does that affect whether I can actually claim it or just whether there's a tax charge involved?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's a good question! Your income level does **not** affect whether you're eligible to claim Child Benefit. You can still claim it regardless of your income.

However, if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. This is a separate tax matter, but you would still be eligible to receive the benefit itself.

Now, let me ask: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex? (Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just to confirm a few final points:

1. Does Alex work for 24 hours or more per week?
2. Does Alex receive any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work 24 hours a week - just focuses on studying really. And no, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- You live in the UK and are responsible for Alex who lives with you
- Although Alex is 17 (over 16), you still qualify because Alex is under 20 and in approved education (studying for A-levels)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex doesn't work 24+ hours per week or receive benefits independently

You can go ahead and make a claim for Child Benefit. 

**Important reminder:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but this doesn't stop you from claiming. You can use the Child Benefit tax calculator on GOV.UK to work out if this applies to you.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently in approved education (studying for A-levels). Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16, but eligibility continues if the child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. You live in the UK with Alex, you are responsible for Alex, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week or receive benefits in their own right. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old child Alex, who is in approved education (studying A-levels). The claimant is responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex does not work 24+ hours per week or receive benefits independently. All eligibility criteria are met.
